_changelog: v1 — Initial build. First lesson introducing heading hierarchy (h2, h3). First challenge that combines tags from multiple prior lessons (h1 from L1, p from L3, new h2/h3). No new validation rules required — uses existing schema v1.2 rules only. heading_hierarchy validation rule deferred to schema v1.3 (see data analyst note in Notion spec).

meta.title: Make it Big or Small! 🔤
meta._xp_note: 3 code_editor steps × 20 XP + challenge × 20 XP = 80 XP. total_xp set to 100 per PRD spec. Open question unresolved — see PRD Section 11. CI XP sum validator will flag this until resolved.
meta.objective: By the end — you will build a page with headings of three different sizes.

steps._bootstrap_note: 45s median — recalling multiple p tags takes longer than recalling a single tag. Three p tags + minimal content. Some kids will need most of the 60 seconds.
prompt_lines: Write a heading with your topic.
Write three p tags. One sentence each.

lines: Did you know the SIZE of a heading matters?
The first heading I tried came out tiny.
Turns out I just needed an h1 instead of an h6.

steps.title: Size = Importance

lines: The bigger the heading — the bigger the idea.

steps._animation_note: Three headings render side by side simultaneously, then labels fade in below each one: 'biggest', 'medium', 'smallest'. Text content should be the same word in all three (e.g. 'Hello') so only the size differs — isolating the concept. Brand colors: h1 in purple, h2 in orange, h3 in teal. After 2 seconds, numbers appear above: '1', '2', '3'. After another second, a connecting line appears showing the inverse relationship: number goes up, size goes down.

correct.byte_line: h1. The lowest number. The biggest size.

incorrect.byte_line: The lowest number is the biggest. Try again.

steps.title: Think of a Newspaper
steps._design_note: Piaget — concrete familiar context before abstract concept. Newspaper is a physical, concrete object most 7-year-olds have seen. The analogy: big headline (h1) → smaller article title (h2) → even smaller section header (h3). This maps directly to how real web pages are structured.

lines: h2 and h3 fill in the details.

steps._animation_note: Newspaper front page animation. Three sections appear top to bottom: large text (h1 labeled 'big headline'), medium text below it (h2 labeled 'article title'), smaller text below that (h3 labeled 'section detail'). Each section fades in with a 0.8s delay between them. Satisfying top-to-bottom build.

steps.prompt: Which heading is for a detail?

lines: Which heading is for a detail?

correct.byte_line: h3. Smallest number — smallest size — most specific.

incorrect.byte_line: h1 is the big one. h3 is the detail.

steps.title: Add a Second Heading
content_lines: It is the next idea down.
steps._starter_note: h1 is provided. Student adds h2 only. This isolates the new concept — they're not writing h1 for the third time, they're adding one new thing below something familiar.

lines: Add an h2 under the h1.

steps.hint:
Type `

` then your words then `

` to close it.

validation_rules._min_note: h2 is a heading label, not a sentence. min:2 is appropriate here — unlike p tags which need min:5 for sentence-level content. A heading like 'Dogs' (4 chars) is perfectly valid.

correct.byte_line: Two headings. Two levels. Your page has structure now.

steps.title: Add a Detail Heading
steps._starter_note: Both h1 and h2 provided as context. Student adds h3 only. The scaffold is intentional — they see all three levels forming before they add the last one.

lines: Add an h3 below the h2.

steps.hint:
Type `

` then a specific detail then `

`.

correct.byte_line: Three headings. Three sizes. That's hierarchy.

steps._bootstrap_note: 55s — bug finding with three different heading tags is harder than bug finding with p tags. Kids need to scan more code and know which tag is broken.
steps.title: Find the Missing Buddy
content_lines: One heading is missing its buddy.
steps.starter_code:
My Dog Biscuit

Why Biscuit is Great

He is very fast.
steps._bug_note: The bug: h2 tag is missing its closing . The h3 tag is correctly opened and closed. Some browsers may visually render the h2 content (the parser will attempt to auto-close before the next block element). The validator checks source pairing: count of '

lines: One heading is missing its buddy.

steps.hint: Find the h2 tag. Does it have a little line in the closing tag?

correct.byte_line: Found it. Every tag needs its buddy. Every single time.

steps._bootstrap_note: 130s median (just over 2 minutes). This is the most complex challenge so far — first time combining tags from multiple prior lessons (h1 from L1, p from L3, new h2/h3 from this lesson). The structure is given as skeleton code, reducing blank-page panic. Traffic light: yellow at 260s (4 min), red at 390s (6.5 min). Do not flag yellow earlier — this challenge requires real thinking about topic organization.

tasks.description: A second heading (`h2`) — one thing about it

steps._tasks_design_note: This challenge deliberately combines tags from Lessons 1, 3, and 4. This is the first cross-lesson integration challenge in the curriculum. Vygotsky ZPD: the stretch is combining known things in a new structure, not learning a new thing. Cognitively demanding but achievable. The skeleton gives them the structure so they focus on content selection (which is hard enough).

validation_rules._rule_note: Schema v1.2 rule. Both p tags must have ≥ 5 chars of real content. h2 and h3 use content_min_chars (min:2) because they are labels. p tags use all_instances_min_chars (min:5) because they are content. This distinction is intentional and must be maintained in all future lessons.

byte_lines: Three headings. Two paragraphs. One complete page.
This is exactly how real websites are built.

lines: Big, medium, small — your page has structure.
